title: `podman quadlet install <dir>` should copy the whole directory
body:
### Feature request description

When attempting to use the new `podman quadlet install` command with a directory, all files inside it are copied to `~/.config/containers/systemd/`.

For example, given https://codeberg.org/herzenschein/herz-quadlet/src/branch/main/etherpad:

```
git clone https://codeberg.org/herzenschein/herz-quadlet/
podman quadlet install ./herz-quadlet/etherpad
```

The inner `etherpad.container` and `README.md` files go to:

```
~/.config/containers/systemd/README.md
~/.config/containers/systemd/etherpad.container
```

This is impractical "at scale" (really more than one quadlet) and can easily lead to issues such as the one mentioned in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26941, which derives from badly named files. In that issue's case, there's no naming conflict if they are stored in separate directories (as long as the container names themselves are different), so at least half the problem gets solved. The suggested solution in that issue solves the other half (though I'd argue quadlet creators should just name things properly...).

### Suggest potential solution

The behavior I'd expect would be to copy the whole directory (which has been explicitly supported by podman for a long while already):

```
~/.config/containers/systemd/etherpad/README.md
~/.config/containers/systemd/etherpad/etherpad.container
```

Not only does it reduce problems like the linked issue, it incentivizes shipping useful docs locally and it would allow quadlet creators to ship configuration files and other arbitrary files needed to extend their quadlets (no need for file extension checks).

### Have you considered any alternatives?

_No response_

### Additional context

This should also apply to things like https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/27068, as it also concerns installing multiple files from a directory.

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I am facing similar problems that the one raised in the issue When using the `podman quadlet install` command I would like to be able to specify a sub directory where the file / directory will be copied. Maybe an argument like `--sub-path` or `--subdir` would work nicely. It would allow to organise the quadlets & associated files when installing them. cc @mheon if this is something that the Podman team would be interested to have, I would be happy to work on this feature 👍 --- I made a quick experiment to show a POC (Available in https://github.com/axel7083/podman/tree/feature/podman-quadlet-install/subdir)

I think, nothing is solved by putting Quadlets to subdirectories, because systemd does not care. For example, if you had your Quadlets in subdirectories, solves nothing because **only thing systemd cares, isthe file name** and only one `etherpad.service` is created and used.
```
~/.config/containers/systemd/app1/foo.container
~/.config/containers/systemd/app2/foo.container
```

A project prefix would be better, let me show two use case with that. **Important note for both scenarios: as soon as I'd start to use `podman quadlet install` I stop to care what is in `~/.config/containers/systemd` directory.** Because it make sense and logic. I also don't care where `apt`/`dnf` install stuff.

As I see on people, this subdirectory thing, remained from docker where everything was `docker-compose.yaml`, so must be separate them. But taste is different, everybody let use the method they they like.

## Scenario 1 - My own local Quadlets

Let's say I have a directory in my home with Quadlet files:
```
~/containers/
|- app1
|  |- app.quadlets
|- app2
   | -app.container
   | -app.volume
```

This is the source that I can install with `podman quadlet install`. With a project prefix (which could be for example the parent directory by default). The runtime location having these files with `app1-` and `app2-` prefixes. No naming conflict (because directories must have unique name). 

On the source side, they are grouped in directories, so if a person read it they can lookup faster if it tastes the person. 
On the other hand, in the runtime library everything on root level, which is handier to handle by software.

## Scenario 2 - Someone else's Quadlets

In this scenario there is somebody who made an app and provide a `app.quadlets` file. I could also install it with `podman quadlet install` with a project prefix. But still, I don't bother what is in the runtime directory. Just like, I don't care where is the service unit for `nginx` when I installed that with `apt`/`dnf`.

But then you could ask, how can I modify it, publish port anything? Same method I'd use with native systemd service, using drop-in directory (see 3rd paragraph: https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-systemd.unit.5.html#description). Quadlet, by design, is made to work on similar way like systemd. 

So if I had a `foo.container` installed from a repo using install command, I want to add customization I'd just add `~/.config/containers/systemd/foo.container.d/my_settings.conf` file (not sure there is `podman quadlet edit foo.container` command similar to `systemctl`).

> I think nothing is solved by putting Quadlets to subdirectories, because systemd does not care. [The] only thing systemd cares [about] is the file name [...].

> But still, I don't bother [with] what is in the runtime directory.

The main point of the issue is that splitting things into subdirectories is a thing you do for _humans_. It is easier for an administrator to inspect their own quadlets if they are grouped into subdirectories, limiting their view to only the files related to that service, instead of having to parse through a long list of files searching for anything prefixed as the name of their service.

People _will_ care about the runtime directory because they _will_ need to override any non-trivial quadlets (or even if they just want to change the PublishPort).

A hypothetical `podman quadlet edit` feature _could_ address this, of course, by abstracting the quadlet directory entirely and have the user just always use the tool to modify their quadlets... or we can have a nice experience when not using the tool, too.

> A project prefix would be better, let me show two use cases with that.

Both of these can be done with subdirectories just fine.

> With a project prefix [...] there's no naming conflict [...].

The original problem in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26941 was that copying something like `db.container` to the root of the runtime dir will always conflict with any other `db.container` from another service. Subdirectories addresses this. So does prefixes.

> But then you could ask, how can I modify it, publish port anything?

Well, as before, you can modify the original file.

But say you don't want to do that, and entertaining the idea of a hypothetical `.d` override feature, this can be done with subdirectories too:

```
~/.config/containers/systemd/keycloak/keycloak.container
~/.config/containers/systemd/keycloak/keycloak.conf
~/.config/containers/systemd/keycloak/keycloak.d/keycloak.container
~/.config/containers/systemd/keycloak/keycloak.d/override.conf
```

This pattern (.d folder next to program config inside subdir) is used by systemd and other system-level tools like motd, ssh, grub, sudo and cron. This should also be simple to parse by machines (it's just another search path matching the name of the service).

---

I guess the point I'm trying to make is that both subdirectories and prefixes can coexist just fine and they both do the job (hell, having both at your disposal would be very useful).

Though I believe we both agree that the current behavior is silly (as I said before, it doesn't scale, and as you said in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26941, it leads to filename conflicts), and I personally believe defaulting to subdirectories would make more sense.

The `.d` is not hypotetical, it exists, see the doc link. Only the edit command could automatically create it, but the drop-ins directory it there for a lot of version already. So you can just create a conf file with you favorite editor.

>The source files also support drop-ins in the same [way systemd does](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.unit.html). For a given source file (foo.container), the corresponding .d directory (foo.container.d) will be scanned for files with a .conf extension, which are then merged into the base file in alphabetical order. Top-level type drop-ins (container.d) will also be included. If the unit contains dashes (“-“) in the name (foo-bar-baz.container), then the drop-in directories generated by truncating the name after the dash are searched as well (foo-.container.d and foo-bar-.container.d). Drop-in files with the same name further down the hierarchy override those further up (foo-bar-baz.container.d/10-override.conf overrides foo-bar-.container.d/10-override.conf, which overrides foo-.service.d/10-override.conf, which overrides container.d/10-override.conf). The format of these drop-in files is the same as the base file. **This is useful to alter or add configuration settings for a unit, without having to modify unit files.**

> The original problem in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26941 was that copying something like db.container to the root of the runtime dir will always conflict with any other db.container from another service. Subdirectories addresses this. So does prefixes.

Nope, subdirectory does not address it. Let me show you why. I have to containers:

**~/.config/containers/systemd/foo.container**:
```ini
[Unit]
Description=Foo container #1
StartLimitBurst=5
StartLimitIntervalSec=90

[Container]
AutoUpdate=registry
Image=docker.io/library/debian:latest

[Service]
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=2
```

**~/.config/containers/systemd/app1/foo.container**:
```ini
[Unit]
Description=Foo container #2
StartLimitBurst=5
StartLimitIntervalSec=90

[Container]
AutoUpdate=registry
Image=docker.io/library/debian:latest

[Service]
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=2
```

Looks fine? From human perspecitve yes they are different file. After a daemon-reload, when I display the file:
```ini
systemctl --user cat foo.service
# [/run/user/1000/systemd/generator/foo.service](file://atipc/run/user/1000/systemd/generator/foo.service)
# Automatically generated by /usr/lib/systemd/user-generators/podman-user-generator
#
[Unit]
Wants=podman-user-wait-network-online.service
After=podman-user-wait-network-online.service
Description=Foo container #1
StartLimitBurst=5
StartLimitIntervalSec=90
SourcePath=/var/home/ati/.config/containers/systemd/foo.container
RequiresMountsFor=%t/containers

[X-Container]
...
```

This is just the number 1 version. The number 2 version is nowhere, I can't even start it. Because when quadlet generator generate the systemd units, it uses a flat structure. Should it also generate subdirectories? Still won't work because the bottleneck is systemd logic! Systemd does not care directories just file names. If you had two systemd units with same name in different directories, you won't have two units just one (based on precedence).

And podman also a bottleneck, because the container name would be `systemd-foo` for both cases but this should be unique. The `docker-compose` solve it with a prefix (which is parent directory or `--project` parameter).

Until you have the same file name in your Quadlet directory, after activation, only just one will be visible. This is why subdirectories are irrelevant in the runtime directory, but file prefix does not.

> The main point of the issue is that splitting things into subdirectories is a thing you do for humans. 

That is also resolved in my previous comment. You have a source directory where you organize your Quadlet as you want, then the `podman quadlet install` place it somewhere. You have subdirectories, you can be happy, meanwhile the `podman quadlet install` manage files as it wants. If you scare that an install would override an existing container unit that is already there with same name, this is why file names must be unique (because even if the command would copy it to there with subdirectories, you would hit the wall with systemd logic).

> The number 2 version is nowhere, I can't even start it. Because when quadlet generator generate the systemd units, it uses a flat structure.

That happens because you didn't use `ContainerName=`. Which you should be doing already, just like with traditional containers. This has nothing to do with subdirectories.

If you are indeed not using descriptive unique names for the container files and you are indeed not using unique `ContainerName=` instructions at all, yeah, the only way to address your problem is by manually setting a prefix at install time.

I go the extra mile and do both because I want my quadlets to always work whether my user is installing them in a subdirectory or in the root of `~/.config/containers/systemd`.

> And podman also a bottleneck, because the container name would be systemd-foo for both cases but this should be unique.

I agree.

Using `ContainerName=` solves this, but the problem still exists for services without it.

> That is also resolved in my previous comment. You have a source directory where you organize your Quadlet as you want, then the podman quadlet install place it somewhere. You have subdirectories, you can be happy, meanwhile the podman quadlet install manage files as it wants.

You are correct that it can **_also_** be resolved by just always using `podman quadlet install`. I said that above.

> That happens because you didn't use ContainerName=. Which you should be doing already, just like with traditionalcontainers. This has nothing to do with subdirectories.
> If you are indeed not using descriptive unique names for the container files and you are indeed not using unique ContainerName= instructions at all, yeah, the only way to address your problem is by manually setting a prefix at install time.
> I go the extra mile and do both because I want my quadlets to always work whether my user is installing them in a subdirectory or in the root of ~/.config/containers/systemd.

I have never specified container name directly neither with Podman nor with Docker, the default ones was always make sense. And it is very straightforward to find the file or service, and less source for naming conflicts.

But I made a test with ContainerName option as you suggested.
The second foo container still not generated and I'm not able to start.

**foo.container:**
```ini
[Unit]
Description=Foo container #1
StartLimitBurst=5
StartLimitIntervalSec=90

[Container]
AutoUpdate=registry
ContainerName=foo1
Image=docker.io/library/debian:latest

[Service]
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=2
```

**app/foo.container:**
```ini
[Unit]
Description=Foo container #2
StartLimitBurst=5
StartLimitIntervalSec=90

[Container]
AutoUpdate=registry
ContainerName=foo2
Image=docker.io/library/debian:latest

[Service]
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=2
```

After a daemon reload, still only one foo.service:
```bash
$ ls -l /run/user/1000/systemd/generator/ | grep foo
-rw-r--r--. 1 ati ati  860 ápr   11 21.21 foo.service
$ systemctl --user cat foo2.service
No files found for foo2.service.
$ systemctl --user cat foo1.service
No files found for foo1.service.
$ systemctl --user cat foo.service
# [/run/user/1000/systemd/generator/foo.service](file://atipc/run/user/1000/systemd/generator/foo.service)
# Automatically generated by /usr/lib/systemd/user-generators/podman-user-generator
#
[Unit]
Wants=podman-user-wait-network-online.service
After=podman-user-wait-network-online.service
Description=Foo container #1
StartLimitBurst=5
StartLimitIntervalSec=90
SourcePath=/var/home/ati/.config/containers/systemd/foo.container
RequiresMountsFor=%t/containers

[X-Container]
AutoUpdate=registry
ContainerName=foo1
Image=docker.io/library/debian:latest
```
It's fixed in #28335. I think we can close this.
